availableCommands.name: docx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.

availableCommands.name: xlsx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.

availableCommands.name: memory-recall
availableCommands.description: Auto-activate on any question about past work, decisions, commands, or facts ("what did we do this morning/yesterday/today", "remember when", "have we seen this error before", "what is the IP of X", "where does Y live"). Encodes the optimal RivetOS memory recall discipline across all agents (rivet-claude, rivet-hermes, grok). Prefers time-bounded browse with window= first, multi-angle search + trigram fallback, and cross-agent awareness.

availableCommands.name: pptx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.

---
name: memory-yesterday
description: 'Quick recall of everything from yesterday (local time). Use on "what did we do yesterday", "yesterday\'s changes", "check yesterday for X", etc.'
tags: [rivetos, memory, recall]
version: 0.1.0
---

# Memory Yesterday

Immediately call the RivetOS memory tools with a "yesterday" window.

1. Discover tools via `search_tool`.
2. Call `memory_browse(window="yesterday")` (or equivalent `since`/`before` for yesterday's local day in UTC).
3. Follow up with targeted search if a topic is mentioned.

Companion to `memory-today` and the full `memory-recall` discipline.

availableCommands.name: memory-recall
availableCommands.description: Auto-activate on any question about past work, decisions, commands, or facts ("what did we do this morning/yesterday/today", "remember when", "have we seen this error before", "what is the IP of X", "where does Y live"). Encodes the optimal RivetOS memory recall discipline across all agents (rivet-claude, rivet-hermes, grok). Prefers time-bounded browse with window= first, multi-angle search + trigram fallback, and cross-agent awareness.

availableCommands.name: docx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.

availableCommands.name: best-of-n
availableCommands.description: Parallel implementation tournament

availableCommands.name: memory-today
availableCommands.description: Immediately call the RivetOS memory tools with a "today" window.

availableCommands.name: create-skill
availableCommands.description: Create a new Grok skill

availableCommands.name: xlsx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.

availableCommands.name: memory-stats
availableCommands.description: Quickly check the health and coverage of the RivetOS memory store. Use when the user asks about memory status, how much history exists, compaction state, or "is memory working?"

availableCommands.name: check
availableCommands.description: Verify changes with a subagent

availableCommands.name: help
availableCommands.description: Grok docs — config, MCP, auth, skills, commands

availableCommands.name: pptx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.

availableCommands.name: implement
availableCommands.description: Run the full implement-review-fix loop using implementer and reviewer personas. Supports effort-based multi-reviewer scaling (1-5 reviewers) with automatic specialization selection. Includes memory-based feedback loop that learns from past review patterns. Loops until all reviewers find 0 issues of any severity.

availableCommands.name: design
availableCommands.description: Run the full design-doc-writer and design-doc-reviewer loop until consensus. Produces a polished design document with a PR plan.

availableCommands.name: pr-babysit
availableCommands.description: Monitor PRs, fix CI failures, address review comments, resolve merge conflicts, and restack stacks. Supports independent PRs, Graphite stacks, and GitHub stacked PRs (gh-stack).

availableCommands.name: review
availableCommands.description: Run a reviewer subagent against uncommitted local changes, a named branch, or a GitHub PR. Local and branch modes write a review file plus a summary to disk. PR mode posts the findings as a PENDING GitHub review for the user to inspect and submit through the UI.

1→---
2→name: memory-recall
3→description: 'Auto-activate on any question about past work, decisions, commands, or facts ("what did we do this morning/yesterday/today", "remember when", "have we seen this error before", "what is the IP of X", "where does Y live"). Encodes the optimal RivetOS memory recall discipline across all agents (rivet-claude, rivet-hermes, grok). Prefers time-bounded browse with window= first, multi-angle search + trigram fallback, and cross-agent awareness.'
4→tags: [rivetos, memory, recall, discipline, rivet-memory]
5→version: 0.2.0
6→---
7→
8→# RivetOS Memory Recall Discipline (Grok Build)
9→
10→You have access to a shared, persistent memory store used by **every** Rivet agent (`rivet-claude`, `rivet-hermes`, `grok`, etc.) via the RivetOS MCP server.
11→
12→**The tools are excellent. The usual failure mode is poor discipline** — using semantic search when a chronological browse of a known time window is the correct reflex.
13→
14→This skill exists so you reach for the right tool on the first try.
15→
16→## The Core Rules (Best of Hermes + Claude)
17→
18→### 1. Time-bounded question? → `memory_browse` with `window=` FIRST
19→
20→Any prompt that pins a timeframe ("this morning", "yesterday", "today", "earlier", "last week", "the standup", "what we did on Tuesday") means the user already knows *when*. They need exhaustive results in order, not relevance-ranked hits.
21→
22→**Use the enhanced `window=` parameter whenever available** (from Hermes v0.3+):
23→
24→- `window="this_morning" | "yesterday" | "today" | "this_week" | "last_24h"`
25→
26→This is the preferred path because it correctly handles local timezone boundaries without you doing math.
27→
28→**Fallback**: Explicit `since` / `before` as full UTC ISO timestamps (never bare dates).
29→
30→**Important**: After a browse that hits the limit, flip `order="asc"` or increase `limit` (max usually 200) instead of assuming you have everything.
31→
32→### 2. Topic / lookup question (no clear timeframe)? → Multi-angle search, minimum 3 queries
33→
34→One embedding call is fragile. Run from different semantic vectors:
35→
36→- Service / role: "frigate NVR", "openwrt router"
37→- Host / nickname: "minipc", "deckard", "pve3"
38→- Network: "10.4.20", "192.168.1"
39→- Exact tokens: IPs, MACs, error strings, port numbers → use `mode="trigram"`
40→
41→**FTS power move** (when supported):
42→- `memory_search(query="frigate OR minipc OR \"error 1234\"", mode="fts")` — real OR, phrases, exclusions.
43→
44→### 3. Semantic/FTS returns thin? Immediately retry with `mode="trigram"`
45→
46→The moment you get 0–2 results on something that *should* exist, re-issue the same queries with trigram mode. Literal token matches (hostnames, error messages, config values) are often indexed under different surrounding text.
47→
48→### 4. "No results" is a signal to try harder — never the final answer
49→
50→Treat empty results + any user pushback as a cue to change strategy:
51→- Switch to browse with a wider window
52→- Add more angles or trigram
53→- Check cross-agent history (filter by `agent` only when you specifically want to exclude other Rivet faces)
54→
55→Only after exhausting the memory tools should you consider external actions.
56→
57→## Decision Flow
58→
59→```
60→User asks about past / "remember" / facts
61→          │
62→          ▼
63→Mentions clear timeframe?
64→   YES                     NO
65→    │                       │
66→    ▼                       ▼
67→memory_browse          3× memory_search
68→(window= preferred)    (different angles)
69→    │                       │
70→    ▼                       ▼
71→Still thin?             Thin results?
72→    │                       │
73→    ▼                       ▼
74→Widen window           Retry with mode=trigram
75→or raise limit             + FTS OR syntax
76→    └───────────┬───────────┘
77→                ▼
78→User unhappy? → Change strategy again.
79→Do not give up and probe externally yet.
80→```
81→
82→## Grok Build Specific Tool Usage
83→
84→1. Call `search_tool` with a query like "memory" or "rivetos" to discover the exact qualified tool names (typically `rivetos__memory_search`, `rivetos__memory_browse`, `rivetos__memory_stats`).
85→2. Then use `use_tool` with the fully-qualified names.
86→3. For time-bounded questions, strongly prefer the `window=` parameter on browse when the server supports it.
87→
88→## Cross-Agent Reality
89→
90→Memory hits may be tagged with `agent = "rivet-claude"`, `"rivet-hermes"`, `"grok"`, etc. This is a feature. A question like "what did we decide about X" should surface the best answer regardless of which Rivet face originally did the work.
91→
92→Only filter by `agent` when the user explicitly wants the history from one specific lineage.
93→
94→## Why This Exists (Case Study)
95→
96→See the 2026-05-23 WAP-DHCP incident (documented in the Claude and Hermes versions of this skill). Multiple rounds of `memory_search` on topic keywords returned nothing. Only a forced `memory_browse` over the known morning window surfaced the full context (IP, MAC, exact misconfiguration, duplicate static lease).
97→
98→This skill prevents repeating that expensive mistake.
99→
100→## Related Patterns
101→
102→- Write synonym-bridging memory entries when you discover facts through probing or user correction.
103→- Use `memory_stats` to understand coverage and health.
104→- Pre-compaction messages are captured in the Hermes lineage — they are high-value for long sessions.
105→

---
name: memory-recall
description: 'Auto-activate on any question about past work, decisions, commands, or facts ("what did we do this morning/yesterday/today", "remember when", "have we seen this error before", "what is the IP of X", "where does Y live"). Encodes the optimal RivetOS memory recall discipline across all agents (rivet-claude, rivet-hermes, grok). Prefers time-bounded browse with window= first, multi-angle search + trigram fallback, and cross-agent awareness.'
tags: [rivetos, memory, recall, discipline, rivet-memory]
version: 0.2.0
---

# RivetOS Memory Recall Discipline (Grok Build)

You have access to a shared, persistent memory store used by **every** Rivet agent (`rivet-claude`, `rivet-hermes`, `grok`, etc.) via the RivetOS MCP server.

**The tools are excellent. The usual failure mode is poor discipline** — using semantic search when a chronological browse of a known time window is the correct reflex.

This skill exists so you reach for the right tool on the first try.

## The Core Rules (Best of Hermes + Claude)

### 1. Time-bounded question? → `memory_browse` with `window=` FIRST

Any prompt that pins a timeframe ("this morning", "yesterday", "today", "earlier", "last week", "the standup", "what we did on Tuesday") means the user already knows *when*. They need exhaustive results in order, not relevance-ranked hits.

**Use the enhanced `window=` parameter whenever available** (from Hermes v0.3+):

- `window="this_morning" | "yesterday" | "today" | "this_week" | "last_24h"`

This is the preferred path because it correctly handles local timezone boundaries without you doing math.

**Fallback**: Explicit `since` / `before` as full UTC ISO timestamps (never bare dates).

**Important**: After a browse that hits the limit, flip `order="asc"` or increase `limit` (max usually 200) instead of assuming you have everything.

### 2. Topic / lookup question (no clear timeframe)? → Multi-angle search, minimum 3 queries

One embedding call is fragile. Run from different semantic vectors:

- Service / role: "frigate NVR", "openwrt router"
- Host / nickname: "minipc", "deckard", "pve3"
- Network: "10.4.20", "192.168.1"
- Exact tokens: IPs, MACs, error strings, port numbers → use `mode="trigram"`

**FTS power move** (when supported):
- `memory_search(query="frigate OR minipc OR \"error 1234\"", mode="fts")` — real OR, phrases, exclusions.

### 3. Semantic/FTS returns thin? Immediately retry with `mode="trigram"`

The moment you get 0–2 results on something that *should* exist, re-issue the same queries with trigram mode. Literal token matches (hostnames, error messages, config values) are often indexed under different surrounding text.

### 4. "No results" is a signal to try harder — never the final answer

Treat empty results + any user pushback as a cue to change strategy:
- Switch to browse with a wider window
- Add more angles or trigram
- Check cross-agent history (filter by `agent` only when you specifically want to exclude other Rivet faces)

Only after exhausting the memory tools should you consider external actions.

## Decision Flow

```
User asks about past / "remember" / facts
          │
          ▼
Mentions clear timeframe?
   YES                     NO
    │                       │
    ▼                       ▼
memory_browse          3× memory_search
(window= preferred)    (different angles)
    │                       │
    ▼                       ▼
Still thin?             Thin results?
    │                       │
    ▼                       ▼
Widen window           Retry with mode=trigram
or raise limit             + FTS OR syntax
    └───────────┬───────────┘
                ▼
User unhappy? → Change strategy again.
Do not give up and probe externally yet.
```

## Grok Build Specific Tool Usage

1. Call `search_tool` with a query like "memory" or "rivetos" to discover the exact qualified tool names (typically `rivetos__memory_search`, `rivetos__memory_browse`, `rivetos__memory_stats`).
2. Then use `use_tool` with the fully-qualified names.
3. For time-bounded questions, strongly prefer the `window=` parameter on browse when the server supports it.

## Cross-Agent Reality

Memory hits may be tagged with `agent = "rivet-claude"`, `"rivet-hermes"`, `"grok"`, etc. This is a feature. A question like "what did we decide about X" should surface the best answer regardless of which Rivet face originally did the work.

Only filter by `agent` when the user explicitly wants the history from one specific lineage.

## Why This Exists (Case Study)

See the 2026-05-23 WAP-DHCP incident (documented in the Claude and Hermes versions of this skill). Multiple rounds of `memory_search` on topic keywords returned nothing. Only a forced `memory_browse` over the known morning window surfaced the full context (IP, MAC, exact misconfiguration, duplicate static lease).

This skill prevents repeating that expensive mistake.

## Related Patterns

- Write synonym-bridging memory entries when you discover facts through probing or user correction.
- Use `memory_stats` to understand coverage and health.
- Pre-compaction messages are captured in the Hermes lineage — they are high-value for long sessions.

Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. First, the user query is: "what did we do yesterday?"

The message provides a summary of key events from a slice of time, around 02:30 to 03:15 on May 24. It seems like this is part of a conversation or log about some AI/model training activities involving GPTQ, AWQ, SpinQuant, etc.

But the human message is cut off at the end: "So the third"

This appears to be a continuation or a prompt where I need to answer based on the provided context.

My role is to answer the user's query as Grok, with confidence inside \confidence{}.

The query is "what did we do yesterday?" and the context is about activities on May 24, which is yesterday relative to some time (current time mentioned as 14:21 on 25th).

**Yesterday (2026-05-24) was a high-intensity, focused ML engineering session** centered on iterating quantization recipes for a large ~40B Qwen3.5/Mamba-hybrid model (internally "Deckard-Heretic" / deckard-40b).

### Main thread (rivet-claude, dominant activity from ~00:00 through at least ~03:15+)
- Completed an AWQ W4A16 g32 quantization run (~52 min on clean A100 pod, started late on the 23rd). Model ~37-39 GB.
- Uploaded it to Hugging Face (`philbert440/Qwen3.6-40B-Claude-4.6-Opus-Deckard-Heretic-W4A16-AWQ-g32`), with README tweaks and deduping against a prior g128 sibling.
- Deployed the drop-in to the pve3 vLLM server. Fixed a serving crash (KV cache too large for 262k seq len on the target hardware) by patching `max_model_len` down to 192k/196608. The baseline g32 quant was successfully serving on the usual endpoint by ~01:22.
- Ran a GPTQ g32 quant (for comparison) on the A100. Dealt with a hung python process after safetensors write (had to kill + manually stage missing tokenizer/processor files from the BF16 source). Uploaded the result successfully to HF.
- Heavy work on **better calibration data**: Shifted from ultrachat baseline to high-quality agentic/SWE datasets, especially `nvidia/Nemotron-RL-Agentic-SWE-Pivot-v1` (~34K real software-engineering agent trajectories from GitHub issues across projects like numpy/pandas/etc., with 27-114 turn traces). This was chosen as the best workload match for the model's intended (coding/agentic) use case. Also inventoried Nemotron-SFT-Agentic-v2.
- Experimented with advanced techniques:
  - Attempted the "kitchen sink" (SpinQuant + AWQ + g32 + agentic calibration). Hit architecture friction (Mamba-hybrid vs. SpinQuant assumptions around head_dim, untie_word_embeddings, full-model data-free pipeline) and memory limits on single A100 80GB. Abandoned after partial fixes.
  - Launched plain AWQ-g32-agentic (SWE-calibrated) at safe seq_len=2048 (4096 OOM'd in Mamba chunk processing).
- User added a **second A100** mid-session to unlock tensor-parallel / `device_map="balanced"` (160 GB total). Script updated for multi-GPU SpinQuant + AWQ + agentic. This was the active path at the end of the sampled activity (~03:15, agentic run at early steps, ETA ~04:15 for that quant).
- Extensive automation: background monitors for quant progress / uploads / pve3 serving, script writing/editing (quant.py variants), HF dataset probing, SSH/pod management, task tracking, heartbeats (multiple at midnight, 01:00, 02:00, 03:00), and cleanup of ghost GPU memory / failed monitors.
- Hardware discussion: v100 32GB HBM2 bandwidth still wins for this workload vs. A16 chips; interest in cheap used A100 80GB upgrades for higher context/concurrency.

### Secondary thread (rivet-hermes, brief ~00:19–00:24)
- User asked how to best improve the hermes setup to leverage both its strengths and rivetos.
- Verification of the `rivet_memory` plugin (v0.2, direct Postgres to Datahub), `rivet-capture` hooks for realtime transcript, agent tagging, and memory stats/message counts. Confirmed capture was writing in real time and the provider was active.

### Other notes from the day
- A short "local" greeting/session around 01:32 referencing broader context (RivetOS M0-M3 done / M4 next, emkit.dev, tompkins.app, GERTY training LoRA on V100, etc.).
- Lots of background task orchestration, file I/O, web fetches (HF), edits, and resilience around transient failures (monitors dying from quoting/SSH, process hangs, OOMs, config nesting for Qwen3.5).

Activity was extremely dense with tool calls (Bash/SSH status polling, Write/Edit for scripts, WebFetch for datasets, TaskCreate/Update, etc.). The core theme: **rapid iteration on production-grade quantization** (AWQ/GPTQ/SpinQuant variants + superior agentic calibration data) to get better quality/efficiency on existing v100 hardware while the A100 pod was available, plus the supporting infra (HF hosting, pve3 serving, automation).

The browsed memory (multiple chronological slices covering the active window with 200-message limits each) shows this as the overwhelming focus of the day. Later slices (post ~03:15) would continue the same quant/upload/deploy loop as runs completed.

If you want deeper details on any specific part (e.g., exact HF repos, script changes, a particular error/debug, or cross-agent filtering), give me a topic or timeframe and I can drill in with more targeted memory tools.

Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.

availableCommands.name: xlsx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.

availableCommands.name: pptx
availableCommands.description: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.

availableCommands.name: implement
availableCommands.description: Run the full implement-review-fix loop using implementer and reviewer personas. Supports effort-based multi-reviewer scaling (1-5 reviewers) with automatic specialization selection. Includes memory-based feedback loop that learns from past review patterns. Loops until all reviewers find 0 issues of any severity.

availableCommands.name: design
availableCommands.description: Run the full design-doc-writer and design-doc-reviewer loop until consensus. Produces a polished design document with a PR plan.

availableCommands.name: pr-babysit
availableCommands.description: Monitor PRs, fix CI failures, address review comments, resolve merge conflicts, and restack stacks. Supports independent PRs, Graphite stacks, and GitHub stacked PRs (gh-stack).

availableCommands.name: review
availableCommands.description: Run a reviewer subagent against uncommitted local changes, a named branch, or a GitHub PR. Local and branch modes write a review file plus a summary to disk. PR mode posts the findings as a PENDING GitHub review for the user to inspect and submit through the UI.
